name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
الٓمٓ (١)
```

```english
Alif, Lam, Meem. (1)
```

```arabic
تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢)
```

```english
The [gradual] sending down of the Book, there is no doubt in it, is from the Lord of all the worlds. (2)
```

```arabic
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۚ بَلْ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ لِتُنذِرَ قَوْمًا مَّآ أَتَىٰهُم مِّن نَّذِيرٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ (٣)
```

```english
But they say, ‘He has fabricated it.’ No, it is the truth from your Lord, that you may warn a people to whom there did not come any warner before you, so that they may be guided [to the right path]. (3)
```

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۖ مَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ ۚ أَفَلَا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٤)
```

```english
It is Allah who created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days, then He settled on the Throne. You do not have besides Him any guardian or intercessor. Will you not then take admonition? (4)
```

```arabic
يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ثُمَّ يَعْرُجُ إِلَيْهِ فِى يَوْمٍ كَانَ مِقْدَارُهُۥٓ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ مِّمَّا تَعُدُّونَ (٥)
```

```english
He directs the command from the heaven to the earth; then it ascends toward Him in a day whose span is a thousand years by your reckoning. (5)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٦)
```

```english
That is the Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, the All-mighty, the All-merciful, (6)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ خَلَقَهُۥ ۖ وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنِ مِن طِينٍ (٧)
```

```english
who perfected everything that He created and commenced man’s creation from clay. (7)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُۥ مِن سُلَـٰلَةٍ مِّن مَّآءٍ مَّهِينٍ (٨)
```

```english
Then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid. (8)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ سَوَّىٰهُ وَنَفَخَ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِهِۦ ۖ وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةَ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ (٩)
```

```english
Then He proportioned him and breathed into him of His Spirit, and made for you hearing, sight, and hearts. Little do you thank. (9)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا ضَلَلْنَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَءِنَّا لَفِى خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍۭ ۚ بَلْ هُم بِلِقَآءِ رَبِّهِمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (١٠)
```

```english
They say, ‘When we have been lost in the dust, shall we be indeed created anew?’ Indeed, they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. (10)
```

```arabic
۞ قُلْ يَتَوَفَّىٰكُم مَّلَكُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ٱلَّذِى وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ (١١)
```

```english
Say, ‘You will be taken away by the angel of death, who has been charged with you. Then you will be brought back to your Lord.’ (11)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذِ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ نَاكِسُوا۟ رُءُوسِهِمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ رَبَّنَآ أَبْصَرْنَا وَسَمِعْنَا فَٱرْجِعْنَا نَعْمَلْ صَـٰلِحًا إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ (١٢)
```

```english
Were you to see when the guilty hang their heads before their Lord [confessing], ‘Our Lord! We have seen and heard. Send us back so that we may act righteously. Indeed we are [now] convinced.’ (12)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَـَٔاتَيْنَا كُلَّ نَفْسٍ هُدَىٰهَا وَلَـٰكِنْ حَقَّ ٱلْقَوْلُ مِنِّى لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ ٱلْجِنَّةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٣)
```

```english
Had We wished We would have given every soul its guidance, but My word became due [against the faithless]: ‘Surely I will fill hell with all the [guilty] jinn and humans.’ (13)
```

```arabic
فَذُوقُوا۟ بِمَا نَسِيتُمْ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَـٰذَآ إِنَّا نَسِينَـٰكُمْ ۖ وَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْخُلْدِ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٤)
```

```english
So taste [the punishment] for your having forgotten the encounter of this day of yours. We [too] have forgotten you. Taste the everlasting punishment because of what you used to do. (14)
```

## (Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to affirm the Origin (المبدأ) — *the first cause and creator of all existence* — and the Return (المعاد) — *the ultimate restoration of life for judgment* — and to establish the proof for both, while repelling the doubts that agitate hearts regarding them, along with an allusion to prophethood (النبوة) and the Book (الكتاب). It then proceeds to distinguish between the two groups: the believers (المؤمنون) who truly believe in the signs of Allah, and the transgressors (الفاسقون) who have exited the raiment of servitude (العبودية). It promises the former a reward beyond the imagination of any conceiver and threatens the latter with severe vengeance through painful, eternal punishment, noting that they shall taste a lesser punishment before the greater one. The Surah concludes by emphasizing the threat and commanding the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to wait, just as they are waiting.

It is Meccan, except for three verses which were revealed—as it is said—in Medina, namely His saying, the Exalted: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ مُؤْمِناً كَمَنْ كانَ فاسِقاً** (Is he who is a believer like him who is a transgressor?) [32:18] until the completion of the three verses.

The verses we have presented comprise the first section of the two sections of the Surah's purpose to which we alluded.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** — *The sent-down revelation of the Book, there is no doubt in it, is from the Lord of all the worlds.* (1) That is: "This is the revelation of the Book." The word "revelation" (التنزيل) is an infinitive used in the sense of the passive participle (اسم المفعول), and its annexation to "the Book" is the annexation of an attribute to the described. The meaning is: "This is the revealed Book, in which there is no doubt."

His saying: **مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** (from the Lord of all the worlds) contains an "excellence of commencement" (براعة استهلال) — *a rhetorical device where the opening hints at the central themes* — because the purpose of the Surah is to explain monotheism (الوحدانية) and the Return, both of which the idolaters deny. This is because, as has been mentioned repeatedly, they do not acknowledge a "Lord of all the worlds" (رب العالمين); rather, they affirm a god for every world and a supreme God for the collective of gods, who is Allah—exalted is He far above what they say.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ بَلْ هُوَ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لِتُنْذِرَ قَوْماً ما أَتاهُمْ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ** etc. — *Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it’? Rather it is the truth from your Lord, that you may warn a people to whom there did not come any warner before you...* (3). The particle "or" (أَمْ) is "disjunctive" (منقطعة). The meaning is: "Rather, they say he fabricated the Quran against Allah and it is not from Him." So He refuted them by saying: "Rather, it is the truth from your Lord, that you may warn..." etc.

Regarding His saying: **لِتُنْذِرَ قَوْماً ما أَتاهُمْ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ** (that you may warn a people to whom there did not come any warner before you), it is said: it refers to the Quraysh, for no prophet had come to them before him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), unlike other Arab tribes to whom some prophets came, such as Khalid ibn Sinan al-Absi and Hanzhalah, according to what is in the narrations.

It is also said: what is meant are the people of the "interval" (الفترة) — *the period between two messengers* — between Jesus and Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). They were as if in a state of heedlessness regarding the rights of Allah's blessings incumbent upon them and the worship for which He created them. However, the objection to this is that the meaning of "interval" is the absence of the sending of a prophet with a law (شريعة) and a book. As for an interval from absolute prophethood (مطلق النبوة), we do not concede its occurrence or that the entire period—which was nearly six centuries—was devoid of any prophet whatsoever.

His saying: **لَعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ** (so that they may be guided) is a goal expressed in terms of hope (غاية رجائية) for the sending of the Messenger. The hope resides in the context or the addressee, not the Speaker, as previously explained in similar instances.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **اللهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ** — until His saying — **أَفَلا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ** — *It is Allah who created the heavens and the earth... will you not then take admonition?* (4). The discussion regarding the interpretation of His saying: **خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** (created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days, then He settled on the Throne) has preceded in similar verses. It was also mentioned that "settling on the Throne" (الاستواء على العرش) is a metaphor for the station of governing (تدبير) all existents through a general, comprehensive system that rules over everything. For this reason, in most instances where the Throne is mentioned, it is followed by something signifying governance, such as His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يُغْشِي اللَّيْلَ النَّهارَ** (then He settled on the Throne, He draws the night over the day) [7:54], and His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ** (then He settled on the Throne, directing the command) [10:3], and His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يَعْلَمُ ما يَلِجُ فِي الْأَرْضِ** (then He settled on the Throne; He knows what delves into the earth) [57:4], and His saying: **ذُو الْعَرْشِ الْمَجِيدُ فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ** (Lord of the Throne, the All-glorious, Doer of whatever He wills) [85:15-16].

The reason for mentioning the settling on the Throne after mentioning the creation of the heavens and the earth is that the discourse concerns the exclusivity of Lordship (الربوبية) — *the divine quality of nurturing, sustaining, and governing creation* — and Divinity (الألوهية) — *the state of being the sole object of worship* — to Allah alone. The mere attribution of creation (الخلقة) to Him, the Exalted, does not suffice to invalidate the claims of the idolaters, for they do not deny that creation is attributed to Him alone. Rather, they claim that the governance (التدبير)—which is Lordship—of the world is attributed to their gods, and consequently, the exclusivity of Divinity—which is being the object of worship (المعبودية)—belongs to their gods, while Allah, in their view, is the "Lord of lords" and "God of gods."

Therefore, when establishing the proof to invalidate their claim, it was necessary to mention the matter of creation and then follow it with the matter of governance, due to their concomitance (تلازمهما) and the fact that one cannot be separated from the other. Thus, the Originator and Creator of things must be the one who nurtures them and directs their affairs, being the sole Lord and sole God, just as He is the sole Originator and Creator.

For this very reason, the matter of governance was mentioned after creation in the verse at hand, where it is said: **خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا شَفِيعٍ** (created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days, then He settled on the Throne. You do not have besides Him any guardian or intercessor). Thus, guardianship (الولاية) and intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — are, like the settling on the Throne, among the aspects of governance.

His saying: **ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا شَفِيعٍ** (You do not have besides Him any guardian or intercessor). The guardian (الولي) is the one who possesses the governance of a thing's affairs. It is well known that our affairs and the aspects upon which our lives are based are sustained by existence, governed by the general system that rules over all things, and by the specific system pertaining to us. The system, whatever it may be, is a necessary concomitant of the specificities of the creation of things. Since creation, in whatever form, is attributed to Him, the Exalted, then He, the Exalted, is our Guardian who sustains our affairs and governs our states and matters, just as He is the Guardian of everything else, alone without partner.

The intercessor (الشفيع)—as previously explained in the discussions on intercession in the first volume of this book—is that which joins a deficient cause to complete its causality and effect. Intercession is the completion of a deficient cause in its effect. If we apply this to external causes and effects, the parts and conditions of composite causes are intercessors for one another to complete a portion of the effect attributed to them, just as the clouds, rain, sun, shade, and others are intercessors for the plant.

Since the Originator of the causes and their parts, and the Link between them and the effects, is Allah, the Glorified, He is the Intercessor in reality, who completes their deficiency and sustains their core. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is the Intercessor in reality; there is no intercessor other than Him.

In another, more precise explanation—which preceded in the discussion of the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) in the eighth volume of this book—His Beautiful Names are intermediaries between Him and His creation in conveying grace (الفيض) to them. For instance, He provides for them insofar as He is the Provider (رازق), the Generous (جواد), the All-Sufficient (غني), and the All-Merciful (رحيم); He heals the sick insofar as He is the Healer (شاف), the Bestower of Health (معاف), the All-Kind (رءوف), and the All-Merciful; and He destroys the wrongdoers insofar as He is Severe in Assault (شديد البطش), the Possessor of Vengeance (ذو انتقام), the All-Mighty (عزيز), and so on.

There is no creature of composite existence except that several Beautiful Names mediate its existence, some above others and some alongside others. Whatever is more specific among them mediates between the thing and that which is more general, just as the "Healer" mediates between the sick person and the "All-Kind, All-Merciful," and the "All-Merciful" mediates between him and the "All-Powerful" (القدير), and so forth.

This mediation is, in reality, a completion of the cause's effect upon it; or if you wish, say it is a drawing of the thing closer to the cause for the actualization of its effect. The result is that He, the Exalted, is an intercessor through some of His Names before others. Thus, He is the Intercessor; there is no intercessor besides Him in reality. So understand this.

It has become clear from what has passed that there is no problem in applying the term "Intercessor" to Him, the Exalted, in the sense of Him being an intercessor by Himself before Himself. Its reality is the mediation of one of His noble attributes between a thing and another of His attributes, just as one seeks refuge from His wrath in His mercy, and from His justice in His grace. As for Him being an intercessor in the sense of His interceding for something before another [being], that is absolutely impermissible.

Because people found it difficult to apply the term "Intercessor" to Him, the Exalted, in the second sense—meaning Him being an intercessor before another—they differed in the interpretation of the verse into several views:

Some said: "besides" (دون) in His saying: **ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا شَفِيعٍ** means "with" (عند), and "besides Him" (مِنْ دُونِهِ) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pronoun in "for you" (لَكُمْ). The meaning would be: "You have no guardian or intercessor from Him while you are bypassing Him," meaning you have no guardian or intercessor with Allah.

The objection to this is that although "besides" (دون) can correctly mean "with," the presence of "from" (مِنْ) is a contextual clue that it means "other than" (غير). There is no sense in taking it as "bypassing" or returning the meaning of "you have none besides Him" to "you have none with Him."

Others said: "Intercessor" in the verse is used metaphorically to mean "helper" (الناصر), and "besides" means "other than," and "besides Him" is a circumstantial qualifier for "guardian" (وَلِيٍّ). The meaning would be: "You have no guardian or helper other than Him." The objection is that this is a metaphorical usage without necessity.

Others said: the use of "Intercessor" here is a case of "estimated correspondence" (مشاكلة تقديرية) — *a rhetorical device where a term is used because it was used by the opponent* — because the polytheists being warned often said regarding their idols: "These are our intercessors," and they claimed that each of them was an intercessor for them. The meaning, based on this, is: "If it were assumed and estimated that a god is a guardian and intercessor, you have no guardian or intercessor other than Allah, the Glorified."

Others said: "besides" means "with," and the pronoun in "besides Him" refers to the punishment. The meaning would be: "You have no guardian besides His punishment," meaning a relative who benefits you and repels His punishment from you, nor an intercessor who intercedes for you.

The objection is that returning the pronoun to "punishment" is an arbitrary claim without evidence. All these views are undermined by the fact that they are forced interpretations arising from taking the intercessor as being distinct from the one before whom intercession is made. You have already learned that the meaning is analytical, and the Intercessor and the One before whom intercession is made are one and the same.

His saying: **أَفَلا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ** (will you not then take admonition?) is a reproachful interrogation (استفهام توبيخي) rebuking them for their persistence in turning away from the proofs of reason, so that they might remember that sovereignty and governance belong to Allah, the Glorified, and He is the Truthful Object of Worship; they have no guardian or intercessor besides Him, as they claim for their idols.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ مِنَ السَّماءِ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ ثُمَّ يَعْرُجُ إِلَيْهِ فِي يَوْمٍ كانَ مِقْدارُهُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ مِمَّا تَعُدُّونَ** — *He directs the command from the heaven to the earth; then it ascends to Him in a day whose span is a thousand years by your reckoning.* (5). This is a completion of the explanation that the governance of the affairs of existents is sustained by Him, the Glorified. This serves as the contextual clue that "the command" (الأمر) in the verse refers to the "affair" or "matter" (الشأن), not the "command" that is the opposite of a prohibition.

Governance (التدبير) is to place a thing after another (وضع الشيء في دابر الشيء) and to bring about one matter after another. It refers back to the manifestation of the existence of events one after another, like a continuous chain between the heaven and the earth. Allah, the Exalted, has said: **وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ** (There is not a thing but that its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure) [18:21], and He said: **إِنَّا كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْناهُ بِقَدَرٍ** (Indeed We have created everything in a measure) [54:49].

His saying: **ثُمَّ يَعْرُجُ إِلَيْهِ** (then it ascends to Him) after His saying: **يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ مِنَ السَّماءِ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ** (He directs the command from the heaven to the earth) is not devoid of an indication that "directs" (يُدَبِّرُ) implies the meaning of "sending down" (التنزيل). The meaning is: "He directs the command while sending it down—or He sends it down while directing it—from the heaven to the earth." Perhaps it is the "command" alluded to in His saying: **فَقَضاهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ وَأَوْحى فِي كُلِّ سَماءٍ أَمْرَها** (So He completed them as seven heavens in two days and revealed to every heaven its command) [41:12].

In His saying: **يَعْرُجُ إِلَيْهِ** (it ascends to Him) there is an indication that what is meant by "the heaven" (السماء) is the station of proximity (مقام القرب) where the reins of all affairs terminate, rather than the heaven in the sense of the upward direction or a region of the physical world. This is because the command was described as "descending" before its "ascent." The apparent meaning of ascent is a rising through the path from which it descended. Nothing was mentioned there except a high point, which is the heaven, and a low point, which is the earth, along with a descent and an ascent. The descent from the heaven and the ascent to Allah suggests that the heaven is the station of Presence (مقام الحضور) from which the governance of the command issues, or that the locus of governing earthly affairs is the heaven, and Allah, who encompasses all things, sends down the earthly governance from this locus. Perhaps this is closer to understanding in light of His saying: **وَأَوْحى فِي كُلِّ سَماءٍ أَمْرَها** (and revealed to every heaven its command).

His saying: **فِي يَوْمٍ كانَ مِقْدارُهُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ مِمَّا تَعُدُّونَ** (in a day whose span is a thousand years by your reckoning) means, in any case, that it occurs within a vessel [of time] which, if applied to the time of events and the measure of their movement on earth, would correspond to a thousand years of what we count. For it is established that the time measured by our counting of night, day, months, and years does not exceed the earthly world.

Since what is meant by "the heaven" is the realm of proximity and Presence—which is something time has no access to—the meaning is that it is a container which, if applied to the measure of the movement of events on earth, its span would be a thousand years of what you count.

As for whether this span is the measure of the descent, the stay, and the ascent; or the measure of the sum of the descent and ascent without the stay; or the measure of each one of the descent and ascent; or the measure of the ascent itself only—based on "in a day" being a qualifier for His saying "it ascends to Him" only, as occurs in His saying: **تَعْرُجُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ فِي يَوْمٍ كانَ مِقْدارُهُ خَمْسِينَ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ** (The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him in a day whose span is fifty thousand years) [70:4].

Furthermore, assuming the vessel is a qualifier for the ascent, is the ascent the absolute ascent of events to Allah, or the ascent on the Day of Resurrection, being the span of the Day of Resurrection? As for it being fifty thousand years, that is in relation to the disbeliever in terms of hardship; or perhaps the thousand years is the span of one of the scenes of the Day of Resurrection, which consists of fifty stations, each station being a thousand years.

Then, is the meaning of His saying: **مِقْدارُهُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ** (its span is a thousand years) a literal delimitation, or is it intended merely to signify a great number, as in His saying: **يَوَدُّ أَحَدُهُمْ لَوْ يُعَمَّرُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ** (Each of them is eager to be given a life of a thousand years) [2:96], meaning to be given a very long life, even if this possibility is far from the context?

The verse—as you see—admits all these possibilities, and each has a basis. The closest of them to the mind is that "in a day" is a qualifier for His saying: **ثُمَّ يَعْرُجُ إِلَيْهِ** (then it ascends to Him), and that what is meant by the day of the command's ascent is one scene among the fifty scenes of the Day of Resurrection. And Allah knows best.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **ذلِكَ عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ** — *That is the Knower of the Unseen and the manifest, the All-mighty, the All-merciful* (6). The interpretation of the individual words of the verse has preceded, and the suitability of these three noble Names to the context is apparent.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ** — *who perfected everything He created* (7). Al-Raghib said: "Beauty (الحسن) is an expression for everything that is pleasing (مبهج)—in the active participle form—and desired. This is of three types: that which is deemed good by reason, that which is deemed good by passion, and that which is deemed good by the senses." (End quote). This is a definition of it by its property and its division according to the divisions of human perceptions.

Its reality is the harmony (ملاءمة) of the parts of a thing with one another, and the harmony of the whole with its external purpose and goal. Thus, the beauty of a face is the harmony of its parts—the eye, eyebrow, nose, mouth, and others. The beauty of justice is its harmony with the purpose of civil society, which is for every possessor of a right to obtain his right, and so on.

Contemplation of the creation of things—each of which is harmonious in its parts within itself, and the whole of its existence is equipped with what suits its perfection and happiness in a manner that is most complete and perfect—reveals that each of them is beautiful in itself with a beauty that is most complete and perfect in view of itself.

As for the evil and ugliness we see in things, it is due to one of two matters: either because the evil thing possesses a privative (عدمي) title to which the evil returns, not to its existence in itself—such as injustice (الظلم) and adultery (الزنا). Injustice is not evil and ugly insofar as it is an act among acts, but insofar as it nullifies a fixed right. Adultery is not evil and ugly from the perspective of the external physical act itself—which is shared between it and marriage—but insofar as it involves a violation of a religious prohibition or social interest.

Or, it is by comparison to something else, whereby evil and ugliness occur through the path of comparison, such as comparing the colocynth to the melon, the thorn to the rose, or the scorpion to the human. Evil only occurs to these things through the path of comparison to their opposites and then comparing them to our nature. This type of evil returns, in reality, to the first type.

In any case, a thing, insofar as it is an existent (موجود) and a creature (مخلوق), is not characterized by evil. This is indicated by the verse: **الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ** (who perfected everything He created) when joined with His saying: **اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** (Allah is the creator of all things) [40:62]. They yield two results: first, that creation is concomitant with beauty, so every creature is beautiful insofar as it is a creature.

Second, that every evil and ugly thing is not a "creature" insofar as it is evil and ugly—such as sins and misdeeds insofar as they are sins and misdeeds, and things that are evil from the perspective of comparison.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنْسانِ مِنْ طِينٍ** — *and He began the creation of man from clay* (7). What is meant by "man" (الإنسان) is the species. Thus, the one whose creation was begun from clay is the species whose individuals terminate at the one who was created from clay without procreation from a father and mother, such as Adam and his spouse (peace be upon them). The proof for this is His saying after it: **ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِنْ سُلالَةٍ مِنْ ماءٍ مَهِينٍ** (then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid). Progeny (النسل) is birth through the separation of the offspring from the parents. The contrast between the "beginning of creation" and "progeny" does not suit the interpretation that the "beginning of creation" refers to the beginning of the creation of the human created from a base fluid. If that were intended, it would have been proper to say: "then He made him an extract from a base fluid." So understand this.

Regarding His saying: **ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِنْ سُلالَةٍ مِنْ ماءٍ مَهِينٍ** (then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid) (8). The **extract** (السلالة), as in *al-Majma'*, is the "purest part" (الصفوة) that is "extracted" (تنسل)—meaning pulled out—from something else. The fluid of a man is called an extract because it is extracted from his loins. **Base** (المهين) is from *hawn*, which is weakness and insignificance. The particle "then" (ثُمَّ) signifies a temporal delay (تراخي زماني).

The meaning is: "Then He made his birth by way of separation from a pure part of a weak or insignificant fluid."

**His saying, the Exalted**: **ثُمَّ سَوَّاهُ وَنَفَخَ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِهِ** — *Then He proportioned him and breathed into him of His spirit* (9). Proportioning (التسوية) is the shaping and completion of the work. In His saying: **نَفَخَ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِهِ** (breathed into him of His spirit) there is a "metaphor by allusion" (استعارة بالكناية) by likening the spirit to the breath (النفس) that one breathes, which is then blown into the mold of the one He proportioned. The annexation of the spirit to Him, the Exalted, is an "honorific annexation" (إضافة تشريفية). The meaning is: "Then He shaped the human whose creation was begun from clay and whose progeny was made from an extract of a base fluid, and He breathed into him of a noble spirit attributed to Him, the Exalted."

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ قَلِيلاً ما تَشْكُرُونَ** — *and He made for you hearing, eyesight, and hearts. Little is the thanks you give!* (9). This is an expression of favor regarding the blessing of sensory and intellectual perception. Hearing and sight are for the sensibles, and hearts (الأفئدة) are for the intellectuals, encompassing both particular imaginary perceptions and universal rational perceptions.

His saying: **قَلِيلاً ما تَشْكُرُونَ** (Little is the thanks you give!) means: "you give but little thanks." The sentence is parenthetical (اعتراضية) in the position of reproach. It is also said: the sentence is a circumstantial qualifier (حالية). The meaning would be: "He made for you eyesight and hearts while you give little thanks." In any case, the sentence is intended for lamentation, complaint, and reproach.

The shift (الالتفات) in His saying: **وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ** (and He made for you) etc., from the third person to the second person plural address is to record that the divine favor, which encompasses everyone, exceeds their gratitude; thus they are falling short, or most of them are negligent.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَقالُوا أَإِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ بَلْ هُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كافِرُونَ** — *They say, ‘When we have been lost in the earth, shall we indeed be [recreated] in a new creation?’ Rather they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord.* (10). This is an argument from the deniers of the Resurrection based on "deeming it improbable" (استبعاد). Being "lost in the earth" (الضلال في الأرض) is said to mean "perishing" (الضيعة), as it is said: "the blessing was lost," meaning it perished. It is also said to mean "disappearance" (الغيبة). In any case, their meaning is: "When we have died and the parts of our bodies have scattered in the earth, such that our parts are not distinguished from other parts of the earth and there is no news of us, shall we then fall into a new creation and be created a second time as our first creation?"

The interrogation is for denial (الإنكار), and the "new creation" is the Resurrection.

His saying: **بَلْ هُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كافِرُونَ** (Rather they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord) is a "digression" (إضراب) from the implication of their saying: "When we have been lost in the earth." It is as if it were said: "They do not reject the new creation because they reject Our power over that, or for some other reason; rather, they are disbelievers in the return to Us and our encounter." Therefore, the answer to their saying was given with that which indicates the Return.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ** — *Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you fully, then you will be brought back to your Lord.’* (11). To "take fully" (توفي) a thing is to take it completely and entirely, like "taking fully" a right or "taking fully" a debt from a debtor (المديون).

Regarding His saying: **مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ** (the angel of death, who has been given charge of you), it is said: it means he is given charge of causing your death and seizing your souls. The verse is absolute and apparently refers to more general than that.

The "taking fully" (التوفي) is attributed in this verse to the angel of death; in His saying: **اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها** (Allah takes the souls fully at the time of their death) [39:42] to Him, the Exalted; in His saying: **حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا** (until when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him fully) [6:61]; and in His saying: **الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ** (those whom the angels take fully while they are wronging themselves) [16:28] to the messengers and the angels. This is in view of the different levels of causes. The proximate cause (السبب القريب) is the angels, the messengers, who are the assistants of the angel of death. Above them is the angel of death, the commander who executes the command of Allah. And Allah is behind them, encompassing all; He is the Supreme Cause and the Causer of causes. This is, in a way, like a person writing with a pen: the pen is a writer, the hand is a writer, and the person is a writer.

His saying: **ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ** (then you will be brought back to your Lord) is the return which was expressed in the previous verse as the "encounter" (اللقاء). Its locus is the Resurrection, which follows the "taking fully" and is delayed from it, as indicated by the conjunction "then" (ثُمَّ) which signifies delay.

The verse—in any case—is an answer to the argument based on the "loss" of the dead in the earth to negate the Resurrection. It is well known that the angel of death causing them to die does not, by itself, resolve the substance of the problem. Thus, His saying: "then you will be brought back to your Lord" would remain a claim devoid of proof in the face of their reasoned claim. Divine speech is far too exalted to engage in this type of argumentation.

However, He, the Exalted, commanded His Messenger to answer their argument—which is based on deeming it improbable—by stating that the reality of death is not an annihilation for you nor a "loss" of you in the earth. Rather, the angel of death who is given charge of you takes you "fully" (تامين كاملين) from your bodies—meaning he extracts your souls from your bodies, in the sense of severing their connection to the bodies. Your souls are the entirety of your reality. Thus, you—meaning what is signified by the pronoun "you" (كم)—are preserved; nothing of you is lost in the earth. It is only the bodies that are lost and change from state to state, and they were already subject to change from the beginning of their existence. Furthermore, you are preserved until you return to your Lord through the Resurrection and the return of the souls to their bodies.

By this, their argument for negating the Return based on their "loss" is repelled, whether it is formulated as a matter of improbability or on the basis that the disintegration of the body nullifies the personhood of the human, such that he ceases to exist, and there is no meaning to the restoration of the non-existent. For the reality of the human is his self (نفسه) which he refers to when saying "I," and it is other than the body; the body follows it in its personhood. The self does not disintegrate with death nor does it cease to exist; rather, it is preserved in the power of Allah until permission is given for its return to its Lord for reckoning and reward. Thus, he is resurrected according to the condition mentioned by Allah, the Glorified.

From what has preceded, the following has become clear: First, the reason for the connection of His saying: **قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ** (Say, ‘He will take you fully’) etc., to His saying: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ مُؤْمِناً** (Is he who is a believer...) etc., and that it is a decisive answer to the problem and a cutting off of the doubt. The matter was difficult for some who interpreted "taking fully" (التوفي) as absolute causing of death without noticing the nuance of the expression "taking fully," so they struggled to justify the connection between the two verses in a way that sound reason would not accept.

Second: that the verse is among the clearest Quranic verses indicating the "immateriality of the soul" (تجرد النفس) — *the soul's existence as a non-physical entity* — in the sense of it being other than the body or any of the body's states.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَوْ تَرى إِذِ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ناكِسُوا رُؤُسِهِمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ** — *Were you to see when the guilty hang their heads before their Lord [saying], ‘Our Lord! We have seen and heard. Send us back so that we may act righteously. Indeed we are [now] certain.’* (12). To "hang" (نكس) the head is to lower it and bow it. What is meant by "the guilty" (المجرمين), by the contextual clue of the end of the verse, are specifically the deniers of the Return. The definite article (اللام) in it is not devoid of the meaning of "allusion" (العهد), meaning those who reject the Return and say: "When we have been lost in the earth..." etc.

In expressing the Resurrection with His saying: **عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ** (before their Lord), there is a parallel to what preceded in His saying: **بَلْ هُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كافِرُونَ** (Rather they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord). That is, they are standing in a station of the encounter which they cannot deny. Their saying: **أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا** (We have seen and heard) and their request to return for righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) is because it has become clear to them that salvation lies in faith and righteous deeds. They have attained certain faith (الإيمان اليقيني), but the righteous deeds remain. Therefore, they confess to certainty and ask to return to the world to act righteously, so that the two causes of salvation may be completed for them.

The meaning is: "Were you to see when these who commit the crime of denying the encounter with Allah are hanging their heads before their Lord in the station of the encounter out of shame, humiliation, and regret, saying: 'Our Lord, we have seen through witnessing and we have heard through obedience, so send us back to act righteously; indeed we are certain.'" The sum of it is that you see them rejecting the encounter, but were you to see them when shame and humiliation have encompassed them, so they hung their heads and confessed to what they deny today and asked to return here—but they shall never return.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَآتَيْنا كُلَّ نَفْسٍ هُداها** — *Had We wished, We would have given every soul its guidance...* (13) until the end of the verse. That is: "Had We wished to give every soul—whether believing or disbelieving—the guidance (الهدى) that is specific to it and suits it, We would have given it to it. This would be by Our willing, through the path of the disbeliever's choice and will, that he be clothed in guidance, so he would be clothed in it through the path of choice and will, just as We willed for the believer likewise, so he was clothed in guidance by a choice and will from him, without it leading to compulsion (الإلجاء) or necessity (الاضطرار), which would nullify religious obligation (التكليف) and render reward (الجزاء) futile."

His saying: **وَلكِنْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ مِنِّي لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ** (but the word from Me has come true: ‘I will surely fill hell with jinn and humans, all together!’). That is: "But there is a prior, decisive decree (قضاء) from Me, which is the filling of Hell with jinn and humans all together." This is His saying to Iblis when he refused to prostrate to Adam and said: **فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** (By Your might, I will surely pervert them all, except Your devoted servants among them) [38:82-83]. He said: **فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَمِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** (The truth is—and the truth I say—I will surely fill hell with you and with those of them who follow you, all together) [38:84-85]. Thus, He decreed that the followers of Iblis shall enter eternal punishment.

The necessary concomitant of this is that He does not guide them due to their injustice (ظلمهم) and transgression (فسقهم) by exiting the raiment of servitude, as He said: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ** (Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people) [9:109] and **وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ** (and Allah does not guide the transgressing people) [9:80], among other verses.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَذُوقُوا بِما نَسِيتُمْ لِقاءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هذا إِنَّا نَسِيناكُمْ** — *So taste [the punishment] for your having forgotten the encounter of this day of yours. We [too] have forgotten you...* (14) until the end of the verse. This is a derivation (تفريع) from His saying: **وَلكِنْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ مِنِّي** (but the word from Me has come true). **Forgetting** (النسيان) is the fading of a thing's form from the memory, and it is used metaphorically to mean the lack of concern for what is important to a thing; this is what is intended in the verse.

The meaning is: "Since it is part of the decree to make the followers of Iblis taste the punishment, then taste the punishment because of your lack of concern for the encounter of this day, such that you rejected it and did not perform righteous deeds for which you would be rewarded in it. For We have not concerned Ourselves with what is important to you on this day regarding happiness and salvation." His saying: **وَذُوقُوا عَذابَ الْخُلْدِ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** (and taste the everlasting punishment for what you used to do) is an emphasis and clarification of the preceding: that the "tasting" We have commanded is the tasting of eternal punishment, and their "forgetting" the encounter of this day of theirs is their evil deeds.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Nahhas narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "Surah al-Sajdah was revealed in Mecca except for three verses: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ مُؤْمِناً** (Is he who is a believer...) until the completion of the three verses."

In the same source, Sa'id ibn Mansur and Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Ali (peace be upon him) who said: "The 'resolute' prostrations (عزائم سجود القرآن) — *the verses whose recitation makes prostration mandatory* — are: *Alif Lam Mim Tanzil al-Sajdah* [Surah 32], *Ha Mim Tanzil al-Sajdah* [Surah 41], *al-Najm* [Surah 53], and *Iqra' bi-ismi Rabbika alladhi khalaq* [Surah 96]."

In *al-Khisal*, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) who said: "The 'resolute' ones (العزائم) are four: *Iqra' bi-ismi Rabbika alladhi khalaq*, *al-Najm*, *Tanzil al-Sajdah*, and *Ha Mim al-Sajdah*."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad and al-Tabarani narrated from al-Sharid ibn Suwayd who said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saw a man who had let his lower garment trail. He said to him: 'Raise your garment.' The man said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I am bow-legged (أحنف) and my knees knock together.' He said: 'Raise your garment, for all of Allah's creation is beautiful (حسن).'"

In *al-Faqih*, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) was asked about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها** (Allah takes the souls fully at the time of their death), and about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ** (Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you fully’), and about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ** (those whom the angels take fully while they are good) and **الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ** (those whom the angels take fully while they are wronging themselves), and about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا** (Our messengers take him fully), and about His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَلَوْ تَرى إِذْ يَتَوَفَّى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الْمَلائِكَةُ** (Were you to see when the angels take fully those who disbelieve). [The questioner asked]: "Many may die in the world in a single hour across all horizons, whom none but Allah can count; how is this possible?"

He (peace be upon him) replied: **إن الله تبارك وتعالى جعل لملك الموت أعوانا من الملائكة ـ يقبضون الأرواح بمنزلة صاحب الشرطة ـ له أعوان من الإنس يبعثهم في حوائجه ـ فيتوفاهم الملائكة ويتوفاهم ملك الموت من الملائكة مع ما يقبض هو ، ويتوفاها الله تعالى من ملك الموت.** — "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, has appointed for the angel of death assistants from among the angels who seize the souls, just as a chief of police has assistants from among humans whom he sends for his needs. Thus, the angels take them fully, and the angel of death takes them fully from the angels along with what he seizes himself, and Allah, the Exalted, takes them fully from the angel of death."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Ali (peace be upon him) who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) visited a man from the Ansar to inquire after him, and found the angel of death at his head. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'O angel of death, be gentle with my companion, for he is a believer.' He replied: 'Be of good cheer, O Muhammad, for I am gentle with every believer.'"

**واعلم يا محمد إني لأقبض روح ابن آدم فيصرخ أهله ـ فأقوم في جانب من الدار فأقول : والله ما لي من ذنب وإن لي لعودة وعودة الحذر الحذر ـ وما خلق الله من أهل بيت ولا مدر ولا شعر ولا وبر في بر ولا بحر ـ إلا وأنا أتصفحهم في كل يوم وليلة خمس مرات ـ حتى إني لأعرف بصغيرهم وكبيرهم منهم بأنفسهم. والله يا محمد إني لا أقدر أن أقبض روح بعوضة ـ حتى يكون الله تبارك وتعالى هو الذي يأمر بقبضه.** — "And know, O Muhammad, that I seize the soul of the son of Adam and his family cries out. I stand in a corner of the house and say: 'By Allah, I have no sin, and I shall return again and again; beware, beware!' Allah has not created any household of brick, or hair, or wool, on land or sea, except that I scan them every day and night five times, until I know their small and their great better than they know themselves. By Allah, O Muhammad, I am not able to seize the soul of a gnat until Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, is the one who commands its seizure."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَآتَيْنا كُلَّ نَفْسٍ هُداها** (Had We wished, We would have given every soul its guidance), he said: "Had We wished to make them all infallible (معصومين), We would have been able to do so."

**I say**: Infallibility (العصمة) — *divine protection from sin and error* — does not negate choice (الاختيار); thus, there is no contradiction between the content of the narration and what we presented in the interpretation of the verse.

## ( A Discussion on the Initial Existence of the Human )

A discussion on this meaning preceded in the interpretation of the beginning of Surah al-Nisa', and our current discussion is like a completion of it.

We established there that the Quranic verses are apparent—with an appearance close to explicit clarity—that the humans existing today, including ourselves, terminate through procreation at a specific pair, namely a man and a woman. The man is named in the Quran as Adam. They were not formed from a father and mother, but were created from dust (تراب), or clay (طين), or dry clay (صلصال), or the earth (الأرض), according to the various expressions of the Quran.

This is what the verses provide with a significant degree of appearance, even if they are not an explicit text (نص صريح) that accepts no interpretation, nor is the matter among the "necessities of religion" (ضروريات الدين). Yes, the termination of the current progeny at Adam can be considered a necessity from the Quran. As for whether this "Adam" refers to the "species-Adam" (آدم النوعي)—meaning the human nature spread among individuals—or a specific number of individuals who are the origins of lineage and the primary fathers and mothers, or a single individual human in person (بالشخص)?

On this latter assumption, is he an individual of the human species born from another species, such as the ape, for example, by way of the "evolution of species" (تطور الأنواع) and the emergence of the more perfect from the perfect, and the perfect from the deficient, and so on? Or is he an individual human, complete in intellectual perfection, born from a pair of humans not equipped with the faculty of reason (التعقل), thus becoming the starting point for the emergence of the human species equipped with reason and capable of religious obligation, and its separation from the species not equipped with that? Thus, the humans existing today are a perfect species of human whose individuals terminate at the first perfect human named Adam. This perfect species branched out through procreation by evolution from another, deficient species of human lacking reason. This deficient species traces back through successive animal species until it reaches the simplest animal in equipment and the most deficient in perfection. If we were to proceed from there, we would continue to move from the deficient to the perfect, and from the perfect to the more perfect, until we reach the human not equipped with reason, then to the perfect human—all of this in a continuous lineage chain composed of fathers and descendants.

Or does the chain of procreation and lineage terminate by connecting to Adam and his spouse, who were formed from the earth without being born from a father and mother? None of these forms are "necessary" (ضروري).

In any case, the apparent meaning of the Quranic verses is the last form: that the current progeny terminates at Adam and his spouse, who were formed from the earth without a father or mother.

However, the verses did not explain the "how" of Adam's creation from the earth: did supernatural causes and factors operate in his creation? Was his creation completed by an instantaneous divine formation (تكوين) without delay, such that the body made of clay was transformed into a normal body possessing a human soul? Or did he become a complete and perfect human over significant periods of time, during which one potentiality after another and one form and shape after another were transformed, until the potentiality was completed and the spirit was breathed into him? In short, did causes and conditions gather upon him similar to those that gather upon the embryo (النطفة) in the womb?

The clearest evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسى عِنْدَ اللهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** — *Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was.* [3:59]. The verse was revealed as an answer to the Christians' argument for the sonship of Jesus on the basis that he was born without a human father, and no child exists without a father, so his father must be Allah, the Glorified. The verse refuted this by stating that his case is like the case of Adam, whom Allah created from the surface of the earth without a father to beget him; so why do they not say that Adam is the son of Allah?

If what was meant by his creation from dust was the ultimate termination of his creation—like all those formed from the embryo—to the earth, the meaning would be: "The case of Jesus, who has no father, is like the case of Adam, whose creation—like all other people—terminates at the earth." It is well known that there would be no specificity for Adam in this sense such that he would be taken and Jesus compared to him; thus, the meaning of the verse would be corrupted in itself and in terms of its use as an argument against the Christians.

By this, the indication of all the verses pointing to the creation of Adam from dust, or clay, or the like, becomes clear regarding the intended point, such as His saying: **إِنِّي خالِقٌ بَشَراً مِنْ طِينٍ** (I am creating a human from clay) [38:71] and His saying: **وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنْسانِ مِنْ طِينٍ** (and He began the creation of man from clay) [32:7].

As for the view of those who say that what is meant by "Adam" is the "species-Adam" rather than the individual—meaning the external human nature spread among individuals—and that the "sonship" of individuals to him refers to the multiplication of persons from it by the addition of constraints to it; and that the story of his entering Paradise and his expulsion from it due to his disobedience through the seduction of Satan is an "imaginary representation" (تمثيل تخييلي) of his station in himself and his standing in the position of proximity, then his being subject to descent by following passion and obeying Iblis—

This view is refuted by the previous verse and the apparent meanings of many verses, such as His saying: **الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ وَخَلَقَ مِنْها زَوْجَها وَبَثَّ مِنْهُما رِجالاً كَثِيراً وَنِساءً** (who created you from a single soul, and created its mate from it, and scattered from the two of them many men and women) [4:1]. If what was meant by the "single soul" was the species-Adam, there would remain no place for the assumption of a "mate" (زوج) for it. Similar to this verse are the verses stating that Allah entered him and his spouse into Paradise, and that he and his spouse disobeyed Allah by eating from the tree.

Furthermore, the basis of the view of the "species-Adam" is built upon the "pre-eternity of the earth" (قدم الأرض) and the "essential species" (الأنواع المتأصلة), including the human, and that its individuals are infinite in both directions [past and future]. Scientific principles have completely invalidated this.

As for the view that the progeny terminates at a limited number of individuals, such as four pairs differing in white, black, red, and yellow colors; or pairs of humans originating some in the Old World and some in the New World and recently discovered lands where humans were residing, such as America and Australia—

This is refuted by all the verses indicating the termination of the current progeny at Adam and his spouse. For what is meant by "Adam" in them is either a single human individual or the human nature spread among individuals, which is the species-Adam. As for "limited individuals," the wording of the verses absolutely does not admit that.

Moreover, it is built upon the "disparity of the four races" of humans—white, black, red, and yellow—and each of these races being a species in its own right terminating at a pair other than that which the others terminate at; or the continents of the earth being eternally separated from one another, not preceded by non-existence. The invalidity of these hypotheses has become clear today to the point of nearly being self-evident.

As for the view that the progeny terminates at a pair of humans, or more, who separated from another species that is the closest species to it—such as the ape, for example—the separation of the more perfect from the perfect through evolution—

The previous verses indicating the creation of the first human from dust without a father and mother refute this.

Furthermore, the "scientific proof" (الحجة العلمية) established for it falls short of proving it, as we shall allude to in the discussion of the following view.

As for the view that the progeny terminates at two individuals of the "perfect human" (الإنسان الكامل) possessing intellectual perfection through procreation, then their branching out and separation through evolution from another species of human who was not perfect in intellectual perfection, followed by the extinction of the origin and the survival of the branch born from them based on the principle of the "struggle for survival" and "natural selection"—

This is refuted by His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسى عِنْدَ اللهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** (Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was) according to the previously mentioned approach, and other verses of similar meaning.

Moreover, the argument established for this view falls short of proving it. For it consists of "evidences" (شواهد) taken from "comparative anatomy" (التشريح التطبيقي), animal embryos, and fossil remains indicating the gradual change in the attributes and organs of species, and the gradual emergence of animals from the deficient to the perfect, and the creation of that which is simpler in composition before that which is more complex.

The objection to this is that the emergence of the perfect species—in terms of vital equipment—after the deficient one in time does not indicate more than the "gradual progression of matter" (تدرج المادة) in its perfection to receive different animal forms. Thus, matter became prepared for the emergence of perfect life within it after the deficient, and the noble after the base. As for the perfect animal being branched from the deficient through procreation and "lineal connection" (الاتصال النسبي), it does not. This investigation and research, despite its abundance and long duration, has not come across an individual of a perfect species born from a branch of another species, such that it stands upon the birth itself rather than individual after individual.

What has been found of evidence for gradual change is only change within a single species by moving from one of its attributes to another, which does not take it out of its species-identity (نوعيته). The claim is contrary to that.

So, what is conceded is that the "manifestation of life" (نشأة الحياة) has different levels of perfection and deficiency, nobility and baseness, and its highest level is human life, then what follows it, then the next best, and so on. As for that being through the path of each species being transformed from its neighboring more perfect species, this evidence does not provide that by way of "deduction" (استنتاج).

Yes, it necessitates a certain "conjecture" (حدس) that is not certain. Thus, the view of the "transformation of species through evolution" is a "conjectural hypothesis" (فرضية حدسية) upon which the natural sciences are built today. It is possible that it may change one day to its opposite with the advancement of sciences and the expansion of research.

Sometimes this view is argued for using His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللهَ اصْطَفى آدَمَ وَنُوحاً وَآلَ إِبْراهِيمَ وَآلَ عِمْرانَ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ** — *Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah, and the progeny of Abraham and the progeny of Imran above all the worlds.* [3:33]. The reasoning is that "choosing" (الاصطفاء) is the selection of the "purest part" (صفوة) of a thing, and selection is only valid when there is a group from among whom the chosen one is selected and preferred over them, just as Noah, the progeny of Abraham, and the progeny of Imran were each chosen from among their people. The necessary concomitant of this is that there were people other than Adam with him, from among whom he was chosen over them. These could only be the "primary humans" (البشر الأولي) not equipped with the faculty of reason. Thus, Adam was chosen from among them and equipped with reason, moving from the level of their species-identity to the level of the human equipped with perfect reason relative to them; then he procreated and his progeny multiplied, and the deficient primary human became extinct.

The objection to this is that "all the worlds" (الْعالَمِينَ) in the verse is a plural made definite by the article (اللام), which signifies "generality" (العموم) and applies to the generality of humans until the Day of Resurrection. Thus, they are chosen over all their contemporaries and those who come after them, similar to His saying: **وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ** (We did not send you but as a mercy to all the worlds) [21:107]. So what prevents Adam from being chosen and selected from among his children, excluding those mentioned among them in the verse?

Even assuming the choice is restricted to and over contemporaries, what prevents him from being chosen and selected from among his children who were his contemporaries? There is no indication in the verse that his being chosen was at the beginning of his creation before the birth of his children.

Furthermore, if the choice of Adam were over the "primary human," as the arguer mentions, that would be because he was equipped with reason. This would be shared between him and all the children of Adam over the primary human; thus, specifying Adam for mention in the verse would be a "specification without a specifier" (تخصيص من غير مخصص).

Sometimes it is argued for using His saying: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْناكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْناكُمْ ثُمَّ قُلْنا لِلْمَلائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ** — *Certainly We created you, then We fashioned you, then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam.’* [7:11], based on "then" (ثُمَّ) signifying temporal delay. Thus, the human species had an existence before the creation of Adam and the command to the angels to prostrate to him.

The objection is that "then" (ثُمَّ) in the verse is for "rhetorical ordering" (الترتيب الكلامي), which occurs frequently in His speech, the Exalted. Moreover, there is another meaning to which we alluded in the interpretation of the verse in the eighth volume of this book.

Sometimes it is argued for using His saying: **وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنْسانِ مِنْ طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِنْ سُلالَةٍ مِنْ ماءٍ مَهِينٍ ثُمَّ سَوَّاهُ وَنَفَخَ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِهِ** (and He began the creation of man from clay, then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid, then He proportioned him and breathed into him of His spirit). The reasoning is that the first verse, dealing with the first creation of the human, mentions his primary creation from dust, which all individuals share. The third verse mentions his proportioning and the breathing of the spirit into him—in short, his human perfection. The conjunction with "then" (ثُمَّ) indicates the mediation of a significant period of time between the first creation from dust and his emergence in his perfection.

This intermediate time is none other than the time of the mediation of other species which terminate, through their gradual change, at the perfect human—especially in view of the "indefiniteness" (تنكير) of "extract" (سُلالَةٍ), which signifies generality.

The objection is that His saying: **ثُمَّ سَوَّاهُ** (then He proportioned him) is conjoined to His saying: **بَدَأَ** (He began). The verses are in the station of explaining the emergence of the human species through creation: that the beginning of its creation—which is the creation of Adam—was from clay; then He substituted an extract of fluid in the emergence of his children; then the creation was completed—whether in him or in his children—by proportioning and the breathing of the spirit.

This is a correct meaning that can be applied to the wording, and it does not necessitate taking His saying: "then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid" as referring to intermediate species between the creation from clay and the proportioning and breathing of the spirit. Furthermore, the indefiniteness of "extract" does not necessitate generality, for the indefiniteness signifying generality is only when it occurs in the context of a negation, not an affirmation.

Other verses related to the creation of the human and Adam have been used as arguments in a manner similar to what has passed. The answer to them is known from what we have provided, so there is no reason to cite them and prolong the discussion with answers to them.
